name: Andouille Sausage~ Homemade
recipeIngredient: 1 Yard large sausage casing (approximately 2-3 inches wide)
recipeInstructions:
Andouille was a great favorite in nineteenth-century New Orleans. This thick Cajun sausage is made with lean pork and pork fat and lots of garlic. Sliced about 1/2 inch thick, it makes a delightful appetizer. It is also used in a superb oyster and andouille gumbo popular in Laplace, a Cajun town about 30 miles from New Orleans that calls itself the Andouille Capital of the World.
Soak the casing for about an hour in cold water to soften it and loosen the salt in which it is packed. Cut into yard lengths, then place one end of the casing on the narrow end of the sausage stuffer. Run cold water through the inside of the casing to remove any remaining salt. (Roll up unused casing, place about 2 inches of coarse salt in a jar, lay rolled casing on top, fill with rest of salt, and refrigerate.)
Cut meat and fat into chunks about 1/2 inch across and pass once through the coarse blade of the meat grinder. Combine pork and remaining ingredients in a large bowl; mix well with a wooden spoon.
Cut casings into 26-inch lengths and stuff as follows: Tie knots about 2 inches from one end of each casing piece. Fit open ends over sausage stuffer tips and slide to about 1 inch from wide end. Push rest of casing onto stuffer until top touches knot.
Fit stuffer onto meat grinder per instructions, or hold wide end against/handle. Fill hopper with stuffing. Turn on electric machine; for manual, push stuffing through wooden pestle. Stuff gradually until casing is filled and inflating. Stop about 1 1/4 inches from funnel end, remove casing, smoothing bumps carefully. Tie off open end tightly or knot casing.
To cook, slice 1/2 inch thick and grill in hot skillet with no water for about 12 minutes per side, until browned and crisp at edges.
